concepts.title: RC Circuit
concepts.conceptBody:
Analysis of RC Circuit 

I. At time t = 0. 

 

Get the initial charge through the capacitor. And then find the initial current through the capacitor.

  • The initial current can be found by short-circuiting the capacitor, only if the capacitor is initially uncharged. capacitor.
  • Otherwise, apply toop rule to get the current through the

${{\text{i}}_{0}}=\left( \frac{\varepsilon }{\text{R}} \right)$

 

II. At time $\text{t}=\infty $, (at steady state) : 

  • The current through the capacitor is zero.
  • Remove the capacitor, inserting a gap between its terminals and then find the potential difference across its ends.

${{\text{V}}_{\text{AB}}}$ $~=\varepsilon $

${{\text{q}}_{\text{S}}}$ $~=\left( \text{C}\varepsilon \right)$

III. Now, use ${{\text{i}}_{0}}=\frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{s}}}-{{\text{q}}_{0}}}{\tau }$ 

Among four unknown, three can be found using above concept and the fourth can be obtained, hence.

Note: You can also find the time-constant by finding the equivalent resistance between the terminals of the capacitor.

Remember: To find the time constant directly, short-circuit the battery and then find the equivalent resistance between the capacitor terminals.

concepts.title: Charging of Capacitors
concepts.conceptBody:
Charging of Capacitor 

So, far, we have been concerned with the capacitors in the steady state, i.e. the capacitors which have already been charged to their steady state voltages.

 

Now consider a circuit where an uncharged capacitor $\text{C}$ is connected to a cell of emf $\text{E}$ through a resistance $R$.

Applying loop rule

$\frac{\text{q}}{\text{c}}+\text{iR}=\varepsilon $

$\text{iR}=\left( \varepsilon -\frac{\text{q}}{\text{c}} \right)$

Now, due to the flow of current, the upper plate begins to acquire positive charge and lower becomes negatively charged. The voltage across capacitor begins to grow.

$\Rightarrow \text{ R}\left( \frac{\text{dq}}{\text{dt}} \right)=\left( \frac{\text{CE}-\text{q}}{\text{C}} \right)$

$\Rightarrow \text{ RC}\left( \frac{\text{dq}}{\text{dt}} \right)=\left( \text{CE}-\text{q} \right)$

$\Rightarrow \int _{{{\text{q}}_{0}}}^{\text{q}\left( \text{t} \right)}\frac{\text{dq}}{\left( \text{CE}-\text{q} \right)}=\int _{0}^{\text{t}}\frac{\text{dt}}{\text{RC}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ ln}\left( \frac{\text{CE}-\text{q}}{\text{CE}-{{\text{q}}_{0}}} \right)=\left( \frac{-\text{t}}{\text{RC}} \right)$

$\left( \text{CE}-\text{q} \right) =\left( \text{CE}-{{\text{q}}_{0}} \right){{\text{e}}^{-\text{t}/\text{RC}}}$

$\text{q} =\left[ \text{CE}-\left( \text{CE}-{{\text{q}}_{0}} \right){{\text{e}}^{-\text{t}/\text{RC}}} \right]+{{\text{q}}_{0}}-{{\text{q}}_{0}}$

$={{\text{q}}_{0}}+\left( \text{CE}-{{\text{q}}_{0}} \right)\left( 1-{{\text{e}}^{-\text{t}/\text{RC})}} \right]$

$\text{q}\left( \text{t} \right) ={{\text{q}}_{0}}+\left( \text{CE}-{{\text{q}}_{0}} \right)\left( 1-{{\text{e}}^{-\text{t}/\text{RC}}} \right)$

In generalized for any capacitor, we have,

$q\left( t \right)={{q}_{0}}+\left( {{q}_{s}}-{{q}_{0}} \right)\left[ 1-{{e}^{-t/\tau }} \right)$

where,

${{\text{q}}_{0}}$ $~\equiv \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ inital }\!\!~\!\!\text{ charge }\!\!~\!\!\text{ };{{\text{q}}_{\text{S}}}\equiv \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ final }\!\!~\!\!\text{ steady }\!\!~\!\!\text{ state }\!\!~\!\!\text{ charge }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

$\tau $ $~\equiv \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ time-constant }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\text{RC}$

Differentiating the above result, we get the result for current,

$\text{i}\left( \text{t} \right)=\left( \frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{s}}}-{{\text{q}}_{0}}}{\tau } \right){{\text{e}}^{-\text{t}/\tau }}={{\text{i}}_{0}}{{\text{e}}^{-\text{t}/\tau }}$

where ${{\text{i}}_{0}}\equiv $ initial current 
 

Time constant: The time in which a capacitor will get completely charged if it would have been charging with initial constant rate, is called time constant.

$\tan \theta =\frac{({{\text{q}}_{\text{s}}}-{{\text{q}}_{0}})}{\tau }$ 
 

Example

Which capacitors 1 or 2, has the time constant is greater, for which the charging curves are shown? 

Solution:

$\text{tan}\theta =\left( \frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{s}}}}{\tau } \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left\{ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{q}}_{0}}=0 \right\}$

$\tau \alpha \frac{1}{{{(\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Slope }\!\!~\!\!\text{ })}_{\text{at }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }t=0}}}$

Hence $\left( {{\tau }_{2}}>{{\tau }_{1}} \right)$

concepts.title: Heat Dissipated Through Resistors in Series
concepts.conceptBody:
Heat Dissipated Through Resistors in Series 

 

$\frac{{{\text{H}}_{1}}}{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}=\frac{{{\text{H}}_{2}}}{{{\text{R}}_{2}}}=\frac{{{\text{H}}_{3}}}{{{\text{R}}_{3}}}=\frac{{{\text{H}}_{\text{n}}}}{{{\text{R}}_{\text{n}}}}=\frac{{{\text{H}}_{\text{Total }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}}{\text{Req}}$

Derivation: 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{\text{H}}_{1}}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\infty }{{\text{R}}_{1}}{{\text{i}}^{2}}\text{dt}={{\text{R}}_{1}}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\infty }{{\text{i}}^{2}}\text{dt} \\ {} & {{\text{H}}_{2}}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\infty }{{\text{R}}_{2}}{{\text{i}}^{2}}\text{dt }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ so }\!\!~\!\!\text{ on }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{array}$ 
 

But, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{H}}_{\text{total }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\int _{0}^{\infty }{{\text{i}}^{2}}\text{R}$ eq $\text{dt}=\text{Req}\int _{0}^{\infty }{{\text{i}}^{2}}\text{dt}$

The factor $\int _{0}^{\infty }{{\text{i}}^{2}}\text{dt}$ is constant

$\therefore $ $\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\infty }{{\text{i}}^{2}}\text{dt}=\frac{{{\text{H}}_{1}}}{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}=\frac{{{\text{H}}_{2}}}{{{\text{H}}_{2}}}=\frac{{{\text{H}}_{3}}}{{{\text{R}}_{3}}}=\frac{{{\text{H}}_{\text{Total }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}}{{{\text{R}}_{\text{eq}}}}$

$\therefore $ ${{\text{H}}_{\text{i}}}={{\text{R}}_{\text{i}}}\left( \frac{{{\text{H}}_{\text{Total }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}}{{{\text{R}}_{\text{eq}}}} \right)$

concepts.title: Heat Dissipated Through Resistors in Parallel
concepts.conceptBody:
Heat Dissipated Through Resistors in Parallel Combination 

${{\text{H}}_{1}}{{\text{R}}_{1}}={{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{R}}_{2}}={{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{R}}_{3}}=\ldots \ldots {{\text{H}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{R}}_{\text{n}}}={{\text{H}}_{\text{T}}}{{\text{R}}_{\text{eq }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ 

Derivation: 

 

${{\text{H}}_{1}}$ $~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\infty }\frac{{{\text{V}}^{2}}}{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}\text{dt}=\frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\infty }{{\text{V}}^{2}}\text{dt}$

${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ $~=\frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{2}}}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\infty }{{\text{V}}^{2}}\text{dt }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ so }\!\!~\!\!\text{ on}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

$~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\infty }{{\text{V}}^{2}}\text{dt}=\text{K}=\left( {{\text{H}}_{1}}{{\text{R}}_{1}} \right)=\left( {{\text{H}}_{\text{T}}}{{\text{R}}_{\text{eq}}} \right)$

$~=\ldots \ldots =\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{R}}_{\text{eq}}} \right)$

$\therefore $ $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{H}}_{1}}$ $~=\frac{{{\text{H}}_{\text{T}}}{{\text{R}}_{\text{eq}}}}{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}$

concepts.title: Leakage Current
concepts.conceptBody:
Leakage current

 

Leakage current is the current flowing through the di-electric placed between the walls of the capacitor.

$\vec{j}$ $~=\sigma \vec{E}$

$j$ $~=\left( \frac{\sigma q}{K{{\epsilon }_{0}}S} \right)$

$i$ $~=\int ~\vec{j}\cdot d\vec{s}=\left( \frac{\sigma q}{K{{\epsilon }_{0}}} \right)\int ~\frac{ds}{s}=\left( \frac{\sigma q}{K{{\epsilon }_{0}}} \right)$

$i$ $~=\left( \frac{-dq}{dt} \right)=\frac{q}{\rho K{{\epsilon }_{0}}}$

$\mathop{\int }_{{{q}_{0}}}^{q\left( t \right)}\frac{dq}{q}$ $~=\frac{-1}{\rho K{{\in }_{0}}}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{t}dt$

$q\left( t \right)$ $~={{q}_{0}}{{e}^{-t/\rho {{\epsilon }_{0}}}}$

Discharging through leakage current

For this circuit, $\tau =\rho \text{K}{{\in }_{0}}$

This, leakage current can be compared with a discharging circuit whose

$\tau =\left( \rho \text{K}{{\epsilon }_{0}} \right)\left[ =\left( \text{RC} \right)=\frac{\rho \text{d}}{\text{s}}\left( \frac{\text{K}{{\in }_{0}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ S}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d}} \right)=\rho \text{K}{{\epsilon }_{0}} \right]$

Potential drop at any time $=\left( \frac{\text{q}}{\text{c}} \right)$
